title: The Oscars Schedule Changes, and Andrew Garfield On ‘Under the Silver Lake’ | Interview
format: Amanda Dobbins on whether Guava Island and Homecoming count as movies, what the Oscars moving up two weeks does to the fall calendar, and a spring rundown of what's coming. Then Andrew Garfield on Under the Silver Lake, growing up in and out of L.A., and the seven-year cycle he's about to enter.

segments.heading: Guava Island and Homecoming: Are They Movies?

films.title: Homecoming
films.note: Beyoncé's Coachella concert film, directed and executive produced by her. Dobbins calls it a documentary that's also self-mythologizing — the footage is interlaced with her own criticism of the performance she's just given.

films.title: Guava Island
films.note: Donald Glover, Hiro Murai, and Stephen Glover's 55-minute musical, premiered at Coachella and released on Amazon Prime days later. Fennessey: not developed enough to be a feature, not baked enough to be a TV show — a very well-funded web short.

segments.heading: The Oscars Move Up Two Weeks

films.title: A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood
films.note: Tom Hanks as Fred Rogers — the one they both call an outright Oscar play.

films.title: Cats
films.note: Tom Hooper's Andrew Lloyd Webber adaptation, glimpsed at CinemaCon: human actors as life-sized cats in triple-sized sets. Dobbins declares it this year's answer to The Wife — a title they'll be talking about for months regardless of quality.

films.title: Ford v Ferrari
films.note: Matt Damon and Christian Bale racing to beat Ferrari at Le Mans. Fennessey's pick for best trailer at CinemaCon.

films.title: Downton Abbey
films.note: The TV-to-film jump. Dobbins doubts the Academy takes a costume drama seriously once it's made the leap, crowd-pleaser or not.

films.title: Bombshell
films.note: Referred to here by its working title, Fair and Balanced — Jay Roach's Fox News drama with Charlize Theron as Megyn Kelly, Nicole Kidman as Gretchen Carlson, and John Lithgow as Roger Ailes.

films.title: The Goldfinch
films.note: Discussed on trailer alone; Dobbins admits she's still 88 pages into the novel.

films.title: The Irishman
films.note: The Scorsese film they've both been waiting years for — De Niro as a man retracing whether he was involved in Jimmy Hoffa's disappearance. Fennessey doesn't know if a Scorsese movie can hit him the way A Star Is Born hit Dobbins.

films.title: The Last Thing He Wanted
films.note: Dee Rees' adaptation of the Joan Didion novel, one of several Netflix titles they're not sure will get a real theatrical run.

films.title: The Laundromat
films.note: Steven Soderbergh's Panama Papers docudrama, also bound for Netflix.

films.title: Little Women
films.note: Greta Gerwig's follow-up to Lady Bird, with Saoirse Ronan, Florence Pugh, and Timothée Chalamet. Dobbins: the most excited she's been for anything in a long time.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: Opening on Fennessey's birthday. He expects a complicated reception, not a simple one.

films.title: The Report
films.note: Adam Driver's CIA torture-report docudrama, written and directed by Scott Z. Burns, with Annette Bening as Dianne Feinstein. Debuted at Sundance.

films.title: Us
films.note: Jordan Peele's follow-up to Get Out. Both think it'll factor into the Oscar conversation on box office and pedigree alone — they're already hearing pushback against a second Peele win.

films.title: Avengers: Endgame
films.note: Mentioned mostly for its business implications; neither is emotionally invested in who Thanos takes out.

films.title: Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker
films.note: Fennessey predicts a Best Picture nomination as a lifetime-achievement nod for reviving the franchise's box office.

segments.heading: Andrew Garfield on 'Under the Silver Lake'

films.title: Under the Silver Lake
films.note: David Robert Mitchell's neo-noir. Garfield plays Sam, an entitled drifter chasing conspiracies through Los Angeles; Garfield calls him not evil, just a victim of and participant in the city's misogyny — someone who's never gotten what he thought he was owed. He compares the film to a grown-up, one-man version of The Goonies, and says he took the part partly to shake off the discipline of Silence and Angels in America.

films.title: Lions for Lambs
films.note: The Robert Redford film that brought Garfield to L.A. for good — he screen-tested for it after a casting director passed along tape from an unmade adaptation he'd tested for in London.

films.title: Free Solo
films.note: His favorite film of the last several years, brought up twice — once on human limits, once as his answer to the show's closing question.

films.title: The Game
films.note: David Fincher's paranoid-conspiracy thriller, cited as a favorite in the same breath as his craving for real-world puzzle hunts.

films.title: Boy A
films.note: Garfield's own earlier film, referenced by Fennessey as the marker before his camera-acting education on Kavalier & Clay screen tests.

films.title: 99 Homes
films.note: With Ramin Bahrani — the small film Garfield credits, alongside a stage run of Death of a Salesman, with pulling him back from the scale of the Spider-Man movies.

referenced.group: Comparisons in the Guava Island / Homecoming talk

Unmade: The Amazing Adventures of Kavalier & Clay
